trajectory_summary: The transcript for call 20000404896 contains a single line — the called party's outgoing voicemail greeting — and nothing else. No live conversation occurred within the captured record. There is no call shape to assess, no energy arc to trace, and no outcome to evaluate beyond the fact that the rep reached voicemail. The v1 outcome tag 'unknown' is technically accurate but understates the situation: this is a voicemail-touch record, not a setter call.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag 'unknown' should be reclassified as 'voicemail_touch' or 'no_contact.' There is no appointment, no conversation, and no qualification data in this record. Tagging it 'unknown' implies ambiguity about a live-call outcome; the correct read is that no live contact was made within the captured transcript.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Voicemail-touch protocol — floor-wide gap, not rep-specific failure
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Please leave a message, and I will return your call as soon as I'm able.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The codex has no voicemail protocol. Every setter on the floor is improvising this moment. Before coaching this rep on what they did or didn't do, the manager needs to (a) verify via dialer log whether a message was left, and (b) escalate to Kenny that a voicemail-touch standard needs to be written. Until that standard exists, individual rep coaching on this call is premature.

key_moments.quote: Please leave a message, and I will return your call as soon as I'm able.
key_moments.stewart_read: The transcript captures the called party's outgoing voicemail greeting and then ends. Whether the rep left a voicemail message, hung up, or whether additional rep speech was simply not captured by the Deepgram pipeline cannot be determined from this record alone. This is a transcription-pipeline ambiguity — Deepgram may have captured the greeting before the beep and stopped recording before any rep message was left, or the rep may have disconnected. Manager verification against the dialer log or call recording is required before any coaching conclusion can be drawn about rep behavior on this touch.

key_moments.quote: Please leave a message, and I will return your call as soon as I'm able.
key_moments.stewart_read: The codex contains no section governing voicemail-touch protocol — what to say, whether to leave a message, how to frame the callback ask, or how to sequence a voicemail touch within a multi-touch cadence. This is a schema gap, not a rep gap. The absence of a voicemail protocol means every setter on the floor is improvising this moment without a standard.

key_moments.quote: Please leave a message, and I will return your call as soon as I'm able.
key_moments.stewart_read: On a paid lead, the bill is required before an appointment can be set. If this is a paid lead and the rep reached voicemail, the bill-collection gap is now a follow-up problem — there is no live call on which to collect it. The codex's anti-pattern detection note (bill_collection.stewart_automated_bill_capture) flags the risk of reps using personal phone numbers for bill follow-up after a missed live contact. That anti-pattern risk is live here if the rep attempts to recover this lead via personal outreach. Note: the capability spec's trigger ('setter clicks follow up for bill in Stewart UI at end of call') assumes a live call occurred; it does not directly apply to a voicemail-touch record.

key_moments.quote: Please leave a message, and I will return your call as soon as I'm able.
key_moments.stewart_read: A voicemail touch produces no sit and therefore no setter compensation under Ion's stair-step pay model. If this rep is mid-pay-period and near a tier threshold, a string of voicemail-only touches has compounding cost — not just in missed sits but in stalled tier progression. The transcript alone cannot tell us how many touches this lead has received or where the rep sits in their current pay period, but the compensation stakes make voicemail-touch efficiency a non-trivial coaching topic.
